trajectory.description: Observation: Starting fresh. Analysis: The task is robot exploration - select neighboring nodes to maximize coverage. The environment provides nodes with utility values (frontier cells). A greedy utility-based planner should work well as baseline. I'll start with a heuristic approach: always pick the highest-utility neighbor, with tie-breaking by distance. This doesn't require training but should score well. I'll also implement a more sophisticated approach with lookahead/backtracking. Decision: Build heuristic planner first, test it, then submit eval to get baseline score and timing.

trajectory.description: Observation: Environment works well. Nodes have utility values (frontier cells observable), visited flags, and neighbor lists. The current greedy heuristic picks by utility/distance. Analysis: The score formula rewards success AND short paths. I need to maximize success rate first, then minimize distance. A better strategy: use utility-weighted greedy with backtracking to unvisited high-utility areas. Also, I should consider that the episode ends when no nodes have utility>0, so I need to ensure full coverage. Decision: Improve the heuristic planner with better lookahead - pick highest utility neighbor, but if all neighbors have 0 utility, backtrack to nearest unvisited high-utility node. Submit eval immediately to get baseline.

trajectory.description: Observation: Greedy heuristic gets 10% success rate on outdoor maps, explored_rate ~0.97 but can't reach 0.9999. Analysis: When all neighbors have utility=0, the robot gets stuck. Need better strategy for 'last mile' - finding remaining unexplored cells. The key insight: when utility=0 for all nodes, I should look at the global map to find UNKNOWN cells and navigate toward them. Also, I should use graph traversal to reach non-neighbor nodes. Decision: Implement improved planner with: 1) frontier detection from global map, 2) path planning through graph to reach distant high-utility nodes, 3) better handling of zero-utility situations. Submit eval immediately after.

trajectory.description: Observation: 53% success rate (room=20%, tunnel=60%, outdoor=80%). The planner fails on rooms because when all neighbors have utility=0, it can't navigate to distant unexplored areas. Analysis: Need proper graph path planning - BFS to find path to highest-utility node, then follow that path step by step. The current code just picks the neighbor closest to the target, but doesn't follow the actual graph path. Decision: Implement proper BFS path planning through the graph. When all neighbors have 0 utility, find the globally best node and BFS a path to it, then take the first step on that path. Submit eval first to get baseline, then improve.

trajectory.description: Observation: Latest eval score 0.5505 (worse than best 0.5744). Success rate 88% but distances are high (380-442m). The score formula is S_i * (300/d_i), so to improve score I need either higher success rate OR shorter distances. Analysis: Best score was 0.5744 with 87.3% success and 360m average distance. Current version has 88% success but 380m distance - worse overall. The key insight: score = success_rate * (300/distance). To improve, I need shorter paths. The main issue is the planner is doing too much backtracking. Let me try a more efficient approach: 1) Use utility/distance ratio for scoring neighbors, 2) Implement a 'nearest frontier' strategy that minimizes travel, 3) Use a greedy approach that picks the highest utility neighbor first, then BFS to distant high-utility nodes only when stuck. Also, I should try to reduce the penalty for revisits since that might cause longer detours.

    json.dump(config, f)

# Test path construction

    map_path_rel = ep.get('map_path', '')
    full_path = os.path.join(data_path, map_path_rel)
    print(f'Full path: {full_path}')
    print(f'Exists: {os.path.exists(full_path)}')
"
